title: Repairlets marketplace + evented task runner for support automation
preview:
Goal: Ship a lightweight marketplace for small, versioned remediation primitives ("repairlets") and an evented task runner that can invoke them safely from telemetry or manual triggers.

Scope:
- Define a repairlet schema (idempotent inputs, outputs, metadata, semantic tags, permissions) and a signing/versioning model suitable for marketplace consumption.
- Implement an evented task runner service that consumes a canonical event bus (alerts/telemetry/scheduled jobs) and maps events to repairlet invocations via rules and playbooks.
- Provide a UI surface in Console (browse, install, pin, and audit repairlets) + a lightweight API for programmatic invocation.
- Safety features: dry-run/simulate, RBAC gating, scoped secrets (short-lived tokens), automatic canary/rollback policies, and audit logs.
- Developer ergonomics: a CLI for packaging repairlets from runbook fragments, local dryrun harness, and one-click publish into a private catalog.

Success criteria:
- Repairlet schema v1 approved and documented.
- Event-to-repairlet mapping supports rule expressions, templating, and rate-limiting.
- End-to-end demo: an alert -> event -> repairlet invocation with telemetry, dry-run, and audit trail.
- Console listing with install/pin flows and per-repairlet telemetry (invocations, success/failure, cost estimates).

Non-goals: building a public marketplace for external vendors in this phase, or full JIT credential plumbing for on-prem air-gapped installs (these will be follow-ups).

Architecture notes:
- Repairlets are small declarative descriptors + an OCI-like artifact (script/container/wasm) stored in our internal artifact registry.
- Task runner receives events (kafka/pubsub), matches against rule engine (Lua-like predicates), composes inputs (templating from event payload + secret map) and executes in a sandboxed worker with per-invocation telemetry.
- Use existing runbook fragments as source-of-truth for repairlet authoring; support automatic extraction of typed parameters and example prompt sets.
- Integrate with Console rollouts so operators can pin a repairlet version to a team and enable canary gating.

Implementation phasing:
1) Schema + CLI + local dry-run harness (2 sprints).
2) Evented task runner prototype + sandbox executor (2 sprints).
3) Console listing + audit + telemetry integration (1 sprint).
4) QA, runbook import tooling, and internal pilot (1 sprint).

Risks & mitigations:
- Unsafe lateral actions: enforce RBAC and default-to-dryrun for new repairlets; require security review for privileged scopes.
- Observability gaps: ship minimal invocation tracing and add token-level cost telemetry in a follow-up.
- Schema churn: versioned schema and graceful migration tooling in CLI.

Open questions:
- Approval flow for cross-team repairlets (org-level governance vs team-level).
- Execution surface for on-prem: hybrid runner or brokered invocation via control plane?
- Limits & throttles by default (per-team QPS) and cost accounting model for charged runs.

title: Avatar border and sparkline visibility in low-light theme
preview: Audit and implement token mapping and component overrides to improve perceived contrast and visual clarity for small surface components (avatar borders, compact controls, sparklines) in the low-light theme. Current issues: 1) 1px avatar borders disappear against deep surfaces at small sizes; 2) sparklines lose readability when tokenized stroke colors are mapped directly from light theme swatches; 3) compact table row separators and dense control focus rings have inconsistent foreground priority. Deliverables: token adjustments, new fallback rules, component-level overrides for Avatar, Sparkline, TableRow, and CompactButton, automated visual tests, and a brief dev handoff document for runtime engineers on mapping rules and theming flags.

title: Create accessibility playbook and remediation plan for compact action pills, tokens, and modal focus
preview: Audit and remediate accessibility gaps for the compact action pill component family and related token surface (contrast tokens, disabled states, focus rings) and produce a reusable playbook and VPAT evidence package. Scope: compact and dense variants of pills, inline action buttons, small table row actions, and modal focus behavior when those controls appear. Deliverables: 1) remediation checklist and actionable token mappings, 2) design + CSS examples and code snippets for focus/hover/pressed states, 3) regression test harness (Cypress + a11y snapshot tests), 4) VPAT evidence document with pass/fail artifacts and repro steps.

title: Warp-tiler slab-wrap and prefetch index-fence leading to KV overwrite and subtle numeric skew
preview:
Summary: A concurrency bug in the warp-tiler prefetch path can cause an off-by-one slab wrap combined with an index-fence ordering violation. That results in KV slab slots being overwritten during concurrent prefill+decode windows. Symptoms include rare token duplication, subtle numerical drift in softmax accumulators under mixed-precision, and occasional transient degradation in prompt continuation quality.

Steps to reproduce (intermittent):
1) Start a dedicated workload with adaptive-kernel-routing enabled (--adaptive-kernel=true) on an 8-GPU pool and model variant 'redwood-open-16k-quant'.
2) Send a burst of interleaved prefill requests (batch_size=4, prefix lengths varying 512-1024) while simultaneously issuing low-latency decode continuations on other shards.
3) Observe error rate increase and rerun with debug KV instrumentation enabled (RUNTIME_KV_TRACE=1) to capture slab indices.

Observed:
- Rare collisions where slab index wraps to a previously active region (frequency ~1/10k requests under stress).
- Token duplication or out-of-order replay in ~0.2% of affected sessions.
- Numerical drift manifests as small 1e-4 shifts in softmax logits when mixed-precision accumulation (fp16+bf16 fast-path) is used; drift accumulates on long generations.

Suspected root cause:
- The warp-tiler prefetch scheduler uses a compacted index encoding and an index-fence check that relies on a per-warp epoch. Under certain scheduling interleavings, a prefetch completion races with an eviction path that advances the slab cursor; the cursor wraps without the fence observing the wrap, causing a slot to be treated as free when it is still referenced by an in-flight decode kernel.
- Mixed-precision accumulation exacerbates correctness because stale vs freshly written KV slices are combined by accumulation kernels optimized for throughput, producing rounding bias that diverges over many steps.

Impact:
- High for real-time chat and long-generation workloads (token duplication and subtle quality regression).
- Low syscall/crash risk, but compromises determinism and regression guarantees across model variants and hardware.

Immediate mitigations: 
- Add a conservative global fence for prefetch completion on the tile allocator to serialize slab-wrap events (hotfix branch: fix/warp-tiler-fence).
- Disable speculative mixed-precision accumulation for kernels touching the KV slab (flag: disable_kv_spec_accumulation=true) as a short-term guard.

Proposed long-term fix:
1) Rework the warp-tiler index encoding to include an explicit wrap counter and make slab-slot checks atomic with a compare-and-swap on the slot header.
2) Introduce per-request KV epoch stamping so decoders validate that referenced slots match epoch before reading.
3) Add a kernel-level regression test that fuzzes concurrent prefill/decode ordering under representative scheduling.

Next steps:
- (Sofia) finish microbench and stress harness to reproduce reliably by 2026-03-14.
- (Mateo) land hotfix fence and run canary on staging on 2026-03-13.
- Add unit/integration tests and schedule postmortem with reliability and kernel teams by 2026-03-18.

title: Telemetry-first streaming chat handler and retry ergonomics for Python SDK
preview:
Problem: Current Python streaming chat helpers focus on developer ergonomics for event dispatch but lack integrated telemetry hooks and consistent retry/cancellation semantics. Teams building conversational features need: (1) deterministic event ordering and checkpointing for resumability, (2) lightweight telemetry (latency/cost/seen-events) surfaced per-chat and per-token, and (3) simple but correct retry semantics that work with partial responses and idempotent handler code.

Goal: Ship a telemetry-first handler primitive and a short set of patterns/examples that make it easy for app teams to implement robust stream consumers that: resume cleanly after transient failures, emit standardized telemetry points, tolerate duplicates, and surface developer-level signals for graceful cancellation and backpressure.

This ticket delivers: API prototypes, a conservative default implementation in the Python SDK, 3 runnable examples, a small integration test harness, and a design doc for SDK+runtime telemetry contract.

title: Admin Action Custody Flow: Data Residency Locking, Retention Policies, and Append-Only Exporter
preview: Deliver an append-only export pipeline and residency-aware custody flow for admin actions (config changes, role grants, access revocations). This ticket covers: (1) a write-once exporter that pushes signed batches into region-scoped WORM buckets, (2) manifest generation with cryptographic custody stamps and retention markers, and (3) an automated evidence report that exports compliance-ready bundles for legal/forensic requests. Goal: ensure tamper-evident admin audit data with programmable retention and easy handoff for auditors.

title: Cross-SDK resilience guidelines and ergonomic examples for retry/backoff and idempotency
preview:
Purpose: define a single, pragmatic set of defaults and ergonomic primitives for retry/backoff behavior and idempotency handling across Redwood SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go), plus a clear taxonomy of retryable errors and streaming-specific guidance. Goals: (1) reduce customer error-handling copy/paste, (2) make safe defaults that work for most production workloads, (3) provide escape hatches and migration guidance for advanced cases, (4) ensure consistent telemetry and tests across SDKs.

Scope: client-side SDK behaviors for synchronous requests, async/promises, and streaming APIs. Does NOT change server-side semantics except where noted (server may choose to enforce idempotency-key dedup semantics).

Non-goals: mandate server-side idempotency enforcement, replace application-level business idempotency logic, or enforce a single header name for every protocol variation (we recommend but allow configuration).

Recommendations (high level):
- Default retry policy: max_retries = 3 (three retry attempts after the initial request), total_attempts = 1 + max_retries.
- Backoff curve: exponential base with equal jitter. Defaults: base_backoff_ms = 100, max_backoff_ms = 2000, multiplier = 2.0. Jitter mode: equal_jitter as default to avoid thundering herd while keeping predictable median latency.
- Idempotency keys: SDKs should offer a first-class IdempotencyKey helper. Behavior: if user does not supply a key for operations the SDK marks as "idempotency-eligible" (examples: create-resource, batch-write, image-generation jobs), the SDK will auto-generate a v4 UUID and attach it in a configurable header (default header: "Idempotency-Key"). Auto-generation must be opt-out via client config.
- Idempotency scope: document that idempotency semantics are per-endpoint + per-key; do not attempt global deduplication across different endpoints.
- Retryable error taxonomy: define categories (see section below). SDKs should map transport and semantic errors into these categories and apply policy accordingly.
- Streaming ergonomics: streaming reads are generally not idempotent. Provide two modes: resumable streams (server issues stream tokens and SDK exposes resume(token)) and safe-retry-on-open (retry only the initial Open/Handshake). For non-resumable streams, default behavior: do not auto-retry mid-stream; surface an explicit error and provide helper to re-issue request with same "Idempotency-Key" when safe.

Concrete defaults (for SDK implementers):
- retry.max_attempts: 4 (initial + 3 retries)
- retry.per_attempt_timeout_ms: unset by default; SDK should surface application-level deadline but not override global http client default unless user sets it.
- backoff.base_ms: 100
- backoff.max_ms: 2000
- backoff.multiplier: 2.0
- backoff.jitter: "equal_jitter"
- idempotency.header: "Idempotency-Key" (configurable per-client)
- idempotency.auto_generate: true (configurable boolean)
- idempotency.scopes: ["create_job","batch_generate","upload_create"] (documented mapping per endpoint)

Retryable error taxonomy (recommended mapping):
- TRANSIENT_NETWORK: DNS failures, connection reset, connection refused, timeouts at TCP level -> retry with backoff.
- THROTTLE: HTTP 429, server signals (quota exceeded), or explicit rate-limit responses -> retry with backoff and consider honoring Retry-After header if present.
- SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE: HTTP 503, 502 gateway, or upstream overload signals -> retry with backoff.
- DEADLINE_EXCEEDED: explicit deadline/timeouts from server -> retry up to max_attempts.
- IDEMPOTENT_CONFLICT: collisions like 409 where semantics depend on endpoint -> treat as retryable only for operations that are truly idempotent (rare); otherwise surface to user.
- CLIENT_ERROR: 4xx except 409/429 -> non-retryable by default (400/401/403/404 should fail fast).
- SERVER_ERROR: 500/501/5xx unspecified -> ambiguous; default: retry up to 1 attempt (not full max_retries) because these can be non-deterministic; telemetry to flag.
- STREAM_TRANSIENT: stream disconnects or partial-frames -> follow streaming guidance (resume if token available, otherwise do not auto-retry mid-stream).

Edge decisions and trade-offs captured here:
- Why 3 retries? Empirical trade-off between reducing customer-visible transient errors and avoiding high tail latency/cost. Most transient issues resolved within 1-2 retries in our benchmarks.
- Why equal_jitter? Better trade-off between median latency and head-of-line collapse than full jitter for our observed burst patterns.
- Why auto-generate idempotency keys? Reduces surface area for users who expect safe-create semantics (common for REST/Queue style operations). However, auto-generation is opt-out to avoid surprising dedup when customers intentionally rely on unique IDs per request.

SDK API ergonomics (pattern suggestions by language):
- Python: provide a RetryConfig dataclass, IdempotencyKey helper (context manager and explicit param). Example call signature: client.create_job(payload, idempotency_key=None, retry_config=None). Add helper decorator @runnable_idempotent to wrap long-running helpers.
- TypeScript: overloads that accept options: client.createJob(payload, { idempotencyKey?, retry?: RetryOptions }). Provide a high-level wrapper sdk.retryable(fn, opts) and streaming helpers that return AsyncIterable with resume(token) on the iterator object.
- Go: functional options pattern: client.CreateJob(ctx, payload, WithIdempotencyKey(k), WithRetryConfig(cfg)). Return a ResumeToken for streaming endpoints.

Examples (high-level snippets to include in docs and tests):
- show auto-generate: client.create_job(payload) -> Idempotency-Key auto-attached.
- show explicit key: client.create_job(payload, idempotency_key=my_app_generated_key) -> server dedup.
- show retry wrapper: with client.retryable(max_attempts=4) as safe_call: safe_call(lambda: client.call(...))
- streaming: iterator = client.stream_embeddings(...); try: for chunk in iterator: process(chunk) except StreamClosed as e: token = getattr(iterator, "resume_token", None); if token: iterator = client.stream_embeddings(..., resume=token)

Testing & telemetry:
- Shared integration test matrix (mock server) that injects transient errors, 429s, 503s, and stream disconnects to validate each SDK maps errors to categories and follows default backoff. These tests must run in CI for each SDK.
- Telemetry: emit events for retry_attempt (with reason), idempotency_auto_generated, idempotency_supplied, stream_resumed, and retry_giveup. Include dimensions: endpoint, status_code_category, attempt_index.

Migration plan:
- Implement opt-in config first (beta): no change to defaults for GA customers.
- Beta telemetry window (2 weeks) to confirm behavior on customer traffic when enabled.
- If safe, flip default auto_generate idempotency to true for idempotency-eligible endpoints in minor release, documented and announced.
- Provide migration guide: how to disable auto-generation, how to map existing app ids to Idempotency-Key, and how to audit duplicates with telemetry.

Rollout & coordination:
- Coordinate with runtime team to ensure servers expose Retry-After and resume tokens consistently.
- Work with Console team to surface retry/idempotency telemetry in per-route dashboards.
- Notify SDK consumers via changelog and migration guide (email + in-console notification for enterprise tenants).

Risks and open questions:
- Server-side dedup window: need clear contract for retention of idempotency keys and behavior on blue/green deploys. (Action: runtime OWNER to define TTL).
- Streaming resume token format: JSON-safe base64 token recommended; confirm size constraints.
- Default header name clashes with proxies that strip headers; need fallback (e.g., Idempotency-Key-Alt).

Acceptance criteria (must be satisfied before marking Done):

- show retry wrapper: with client.retryable(max_attempts=4) as safe_call: safe_call(lambda: client.call(...))
- streaming: iterator = client.stream_embeddings(...); try: for chunk in iterator: process(chunk) except StreamClosed as e: token = getattr(iterator, "resume_token", None); if token: iterator = client.stream_embeddings(..., resume=token)

title: Helm release stuck after operator upgrade — incident response, config drift reconciliation, and customer hotfix deployment
preview: Several customers running Private (VPC/on-prem) reported failed operator upgrades where Helm remained in a 'pending-upgrade' or 'failed-hook' state and the operator reconciliation loop stopped applying subsequent changes. Symptoms observed across multiple clusters: helm release pending due to a pre/post upgrade hook that never completed, operator pod entering CrashLoopBackOff after CRD validation errors, and configmaps/opaque secrets diverging between control plane intent and node-level state. Impact: customer-facing control plane features degraded, unable to deploy model revisions or scale worker pools. This ticket coordinates incident response, manual hotfix deployments for affected customers, and a durable fix to Helm/operator upgrade flow to prevent config drift and stuck releases.

title: SLO-driven token-budget scheduler with KV slab isolation
preview: Goal: implement an SLO-aware scheduling layer for the continuous batcher that enforces per-customer and global latency SLOs via an explicit token-budget preemption model and a KV cache slab isolation scheme to reduce costly cross-tenant KV eviction. This ticket covers design, implementation, benchmarks, and canary rollout of: (1) a token-budget allocator that gives each inflight request a consumable token budget representing GPU memory + compute entitlement for a sliding window; (2) a preemption mechanism that uses the token budget to decide cooperative vs. forced preemption under contention; and (3) KV cache slab partitioning by tenant/priority band to bound worst-case memory amplification from large KV caches. Motivation: we have observed tail latency spikes during mixed workloads (long-context completions + many small chat requests) where continuous batching saturates GPU memory and causes KV thrashing and expensive kernel context switching. Current global preemption policies are blunt and cause unfair starvation for low-volume tiered customers. The new design aims to (a) preserve p95/p99 latency SLOs for high-priority customers, (b) reduce cross-tenant KV eviction by isolating KV slabs, and (c) improve throughput efficiency by making preemption decisions based on token budgets rather than simple queue age.

title: Heterogeneous phase allocation policy with attention-pressure mitigation
preview:
Background:
The serving runtime currently uses a monolithic phase-splitting approach for prefill and decode that assumes homogeneous attention and kernel costs across requests. As we onboard more heterogeneous models and tenant mixes (short-chat vs. long-context summarization), we observe increased tail latency due to attention hotspots and suboptimal kernel-selection choices when slices contain mixed-cost segments.

Problem statement:
- Tenant fairness and latency SLOs are drifting during microbursts from tenants with few long-context requests.
- Kernel selection heuristics are brittle when a microbatch contains a mix of low-attention and high-attention sequences; this increases overall token cost and latency variance.
- Current scheduler cannot express tenant-level latency contracts without sacrificing throughput for the entire worker pool.

Goals:
1) Design a phase allocation policy that partitions prefill/decode work into heterogeneous-aligned slices (attention-aware) to improve kernel affinity and reduce tail latency for latency-sensitive tenants.
2) Provide QoS knobs to bind a tenant to a latency contract (soft SLO) while maintaining overall throughput targets.
3) Produce measurable improvements: 95th percentile latency down by >=12% for latency-tier tenants and throughput reduction <5% for overall cluster.

Design overview (proposal):
- Attention pressure estimator: lightweight per-request metric computed during prefill tokenization that estimates expected attention compute (features: context length, sparsity hints, attention sparsity flags from model metadata).
- Phase classification: tag requests as LOW/MED/HIGH attention-pressure and enforce microbatch homogeneity where practical (prefer batching LOW with LOW, HIGH with HIGH).
- Kernel hint propagation: attach kernel preference hints to slices so the runtime kernel-selector can pick quantized/optimized kernels (e.g., dense-optimized vs. sparse-attn-aware kernels).
- Tenant latency contracts: a scheduler policy that reserves a small fraction of micro-slices for latency-tier tenants (can be configured per-tenant). If reserved capacity is used, fallbacks allow best-effort servicing for other tenants with hedged decode variants.
- Graceful degradation: when heterogeneity prevents perfect homogeneity, prefer splitting into two slices rather than mixing if token-cost delta > threshold, to avoid kernel thrashing.

Alternatives considered:
A) Strict tenant isolation by lane (high implementation cost, resource inefficiency underutilized).
B) Opportunistic kernel hints without phase splitting (lower complexity but limited tail-latency gains).
C) Precompute attention profiles offline per-customer model and use a static partitioning (brittle for ad-hoc prompts).
We selected the attention-pressure estimator + soft-reservations as a middle ground balancing complexity and benefit.

Experiments and simulation plan:
- Synthetic workload generator: extend the existing microburst simulator to produce mixed attention distributions.
- Offline simulation: run A/B sims of the policy on traces from last 30 days to estimate latency/throughput impacts.
- Canary: deploy to 1 of 10 production racks with canary_enabled flag; measure per-tenant P50/P95, kernel churn, and token cost change.

Acceptance criteria:
- Canary shows >=12% reduction in tenant P95 latency for tenants flagged latency-tier with <5% degradation in cluster throughput.
- Kernel-selection churn (switches per-second) reduced by >=20% for canary rack slices.
- No regression in error rates or increase in out-of-memory kernel failures.
- Monitoring: new dashboards created showing attention-pressure distribution, reserved-slice utilization, and kernel hint hit rate.

Rollout plan:
- Phase 1 (simulation & design): complete by 2026-03-15.
- Phase 2 (internal integration test): 2026-03-16 to 2026-03-22.
- Phase 3 (canary): enable on 1 rack 2026-03-23 for 2 weeks.
- Phase 4 (gradual rollout): expand to 30% and then 100% if metrics stable.

Migration & compatibility:
- Kernel hint APIs are backwards compatible: if downstream runtime does not recognize hints, it should ignore them (no functional change).
- Default behavior remains unchanged unless tenant policies or canary flags are enabled.

Metrics to track (primary):
- Per-tenant P50/P95 latency (ms)
- Cluster throughput (tokens/s)
- Kernel hint hit rate (%)
- Slice homogeneity ratio (fraction of slices with same attention-pressure class)
- Reserved-slice utilization (%)

Risks & mitigations:
- Risk: Over-splitting increases scheduling overhead and reduces throughput. Mitigation: threshold-based split with minimum token batch size and dynamic backoff.
- Risk: Misclassification of attention pressure leads to suboptimal kernel selection. Mitigation: rapid feedback loop from kernel latency probes to adjust estimator weights.
- Risk: Tenant contracts starve best-effort tenants under extreme load. Mitigation: implement soft reservations with preemption policies and emergency capacity sharing.

Stakeholders & reviewers:
- Runtime team: Mateo (owner), Priya (performance lead)
- API/Console: Rana (for tenant QoS UI)
- Product: Leo (prioritization & customer impact)
- Research/ML: Sahana (attention profiling)

Artifacts & links:
- Design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/RUNTIME/Heterogeneous+Phase+Allocation
- Simulator PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/9842
- Kernel-hint spec: https://docs.redwood.ai/runtime/kernel-hint-spec.md

Next steps:
1) Finalize attention-pressure estimator prototype and open a PR to runtime core.
2) Add metrics instrumentation hooks and dashboards.
3) Run offline simulations on 30-day traces and post results to the design doc.

title: Slotted-retirement policy for GPU KV cache: bounded-staleness with prefix integrity
preview:
Problem:
When we mix TTL-driven expiration, LRU pressure and prefix-affine allocations on the GPU, loose eviction (per-key) leads to two classes of production correctness issues: 1) prefix-splitting where a prefix's KV history is partially evicted causing reranked completions to return inconsistent context, and 2) in-flight batch regressions where a request references a KV slot that is reclaimed mid-batch. Both are exacerbated by continuous batching (variable-length micro-batches) and GPU memory compaction strategies.

Goal:
Design and implement a low-latency, low-cost GPU KV reclamation policy that: (a) bounds staleness deterministically (an upper bound on how old a referenced KV may be), (b) preserves prefix-level integrity (no partial prefix retirements visible to inference), (c) cooperates with continuous batching to avoid mid-batch invalidation, and (d) enables efficient bulk reclamation/compaction to reduce fragmentation and GPU OOM risk.

Proposed approach (Slotted-retirement):
- Partition KV allocation space into contiguous time-slots (configurable slot duration, e.g., 30s). New entries are written into the current active slot.
- Each in-flight request pins the slot(s) it touches; slot pins are reference-counted at the runtime layer.
- Retirement happens at slot granularity: a slot becomes eligible when its TTL window (or LRU watermarks) expire AND there are no active pins for that slot. We then atomically mark the slot retired and make it available for compaction/eviction in one operation.
- Prefix integrity: allocations belonging to a prefix are placed in the same slot whenever possible via short-lived prefix-anchoring heuristics so that we never retire only a subset of a prefix's cached KV history without moving the rest. For long-lived prefixes that cross slots we maintain a small prefix-index that records sequence boundaries so we can perform coordinated retire/merge operations.
- Continuous batching cooperation: before initiating a batch, the scheduler snapshots the list of candidate slots for the full batch window and holds lightweight pins on them for the expected batch duration. This prevents mid-batch evictions without adding significant latency.
- Compaction and GPU memory optimization: retired slots are compacted in background worker threads; compaction uses a reclamation plan that prefers whole-slot releases and only does intra-slot shuffles when fragmentation exceeds a high threshold. This allows large contiguous frees for the allocator to reduce fragmentation and avoid repeated small frees that are expensive on GPU drivers.
- Fallbacks: if memory pressure becomes critical, runtime may trigger emergency per-key reclaim within a slot using conservative sequence checks and promoted fallback models for degraded quality. Emergency paths are logged and surfaced as high-severity alerts.

Correctness guarantees sketch:
- Atomic slot retirement ensures that any inference either sees the slot fully present or fully absent — no partial visibility.
- Pinning model guarantees that if an inference references a KV entry, the slot containing it cannot be retired until the inference completes. This gives a bounded staleness: max(slot duration + max scheduling delay).
- Prefix anchoring heuristics decrease the probability of prefix-splitting; coordinated retire/merge provides a deterministic path for prefixes that cross slots (merge is serialized with retire operations for those slots).

Trade-offs and rationale:
- Coarser granularity (slots vs per-key) increases potential memory retention, but dramatically simplifies correctness and enables contiguous GPU frees that reduce fragmentation and allocator overhead — a net win for tail-latency and OOM avoidance.
- Slot duration is a tunable knob: shorter slots reduce staleness but increase background compaction frequency. We will default to 30s and expose slot-duration per-customer tier.
- Continuous batching pinning adds minimal overhead (O(slot-count) refs) compared to the cost of mid-batch retries and model quality regressions.

Implementation plan:
1) Runtime data structures: slot table, refcounts, prefix-index (lightweight), slot-state machine.
2) Scheduler changes: batch pre-snapshot + pin acquisition API.
3) Background compactor worker and emergency reclaim path.
4) Instrumentation: metrics for slot-pinning duration, retire latency, compaction throughput, emergency-reclaim counts.
5) Tests: unit tests for slot-state transitions, integration tests with simulated continuous batching, and fault injection that forces compaction race conditions.
6) Canary rollout: start with dev clusters, then dedicated capacity customers on low-traffic pools, then wider rollout.

Metrics to observe:
- Average and 99.9th percentile pin hold time.
- Number of emergency per-key reclaims per hour.
- GPU memory fragmentation score (before/after compaction).
- Inference error rates and retry counts correlated with retire events.

Rollout safety and rollback:
- Feature gate behind a runtime flag. Default off for hosted until canary validated.
- If emergency reclaims exceed threshold or inference errors rise, gate off and fall back to existing LRU/TTL per-key policy (with a compaction-only helper).

Open questions:
- Exact slot sizing heuristics per workload (batch-heavy vs low-QPS interactive) — plan A is adaptive slot-duration tuned from observed access patterns.
- Whether prefix-index adds unacceptable memory overhead for extremely high cardinality prefixes; plan: sample prefixes and evict index entries LRUs.
- Interaction with model quantization memory layouts during compaction — need closer integration with allocator team.

Deliverables:
- RFC and design doc (link below)
- Implementation PRs for slot table + scheduler pins + compactor
- Load tests and canary report

title: Dynamic padding-squeeze and bucket fusion for runtime micro-batching
preview: Reduce padding-induced waste and improve GPU utilization by implementing a runtime component that squeezes variable-length sequences into tight micro-batches using dynamic bucketing and bucket fusion across prefill and decode stages. The feature should work with existing KV-cache and fallback gracefully for latency-sensitive requests.

title: Observability Governor service: enforce scrub, retention, and access-audit at ingest
preview:
Problem: telemetry streams (traces, logs, metrics) currently apply inconsistent PII redaction and retention rules across SDKs and ingestion pipelines. That leads to support exposures, divergent retention behavior per tenant, and difficulty proving compliance.

Proposal: build an "Observability Governor" service that enforces telemetry governance decisions at ingest time: deterministic pseudonymization / tokenization, field-level scrubbing (regex + schema-aware), tenant-scoped retention enforcement, and mandatory RBAC access-audit logging. The Governor will be policy-driven (runtime rules), pluggable into the existing ingestion path (as a lightweight microservice in the dataplane with sidecar option), and expose admin APIs for policy lifecycle and data erasure requests. It will also provide SDK hooks so producers can surface structured annotations and opt into safe-debug tokens.

Goals: reduce PII leakage risk; provide a single source of truth for retention; emit immutable audit events for all telemetry accesses; minimize token-level latency overhead; preserve observability for debugging and model evals via reversible pseudonymization for authorized roles.

title: Proactive schema sentinel for config & model rollout gating

title: SLO lease transfers and graceful traffic atrophy policy for model endpoints
preview: Context: Redwood runs model endpoints across regions and model variants with different cost/latency characteristics. Teams have expressed the need for a formal mechanism to temporarily 'lease' SLO headroom from lower-priority routes/variants to keep customer-critical routes online during short spikes, then return or rebalance that lease without causing cascading failures. This ticket defines a policy and operational plan for SLO lease transfers and a complementary 'graceful traffic atrophy' (GTA) strategy that intentionally sheds low-value traffic in an observable, reversible way.

title: Localized keyboard focus order and rollout-toast contrast audit for dashboards
preview:
Problem: Several dashboard controls (timeline scrubs, per-route rollout toggles, and ephemeral rollout toasts) generate dynamic DOM order and visual-only focus styles that break keyboard navigation and screenreader announcements in non-English locales and RTL layouts. Color tokens used for toast backgrounds and timeline scrims fall below WCAG 2.1 AA contrast thresholds against localized number/label color variants.

Goal: Produce a design audit and prototype that ensures: keyboard focus order is predictable and stable across localized label length changes and RTL; toast and scrim color tokens meet contrast ratios with localized text and semantic overlays; screenreader announcements reflect current rollout state changes without duplicating messages when language changes are applied.

Acceptance criteria:
- Deterministic tab/focus order for dashboard widgets and rollout controls that does not rely on visual order (use DOM order rules in design guidance).
- Visible focus indication uses high-contrast token pair defined in theme tokens and respects user high-contrast mode.
- Rollout toast copy is announced once via polite aria-live region; for rapid state flips debounce announcements to avoid spam.
- All toast and scrim color combinations pass 4.5:1 contrast for normal text and 3:1 for large text across supported locales (en-US, fr-FR, de-DE, ja-JP, ar-SA).
- RTL layouts mirror focus order and maintain numeric formatting alignment for timelines and cost deltas.
- Updated Figma prototype and interaction spec with keyboard flows, ARIA attributes, and localization notes are reviewed and approved by accessibility and i18n SMEs.

Design notes and trade-offs:
- We recommend reordering DOM for rollout timeline controls to match logical reading order rather than relying on CSS visual reflow. This simplifies screenreader behavior but requires backend/analytics to remain mapping-agnostic of visual placement.
- To avoid visual churn, focus outlines will use a subtle 2px high-contrast ring plus an optional fill when in high-contrast mode. This balances visual density and WCAG requirements.
- For toasts, we prefer a single shared aria-live=\"polite\" region attached at the top of the dashboard root rather than multiple per-component regions to reduce duplicate announcements and make i18n copy changes predictable.

Deliverables:
- Updated Figma prototype (keyboard flows, RTL variants) with annotated token swaps and ARIA guidance.
- Confluence spec describing implementation steps, acceptance tests, and QA checklist.
- Small PR template for engineering with suggested DOM ordering snippets and test vectors (localized strings, long label edge cases).

Engineering coordination:
- Work with ENG runtime and frontend to ensure timeline scrubbing remains performant when DOM order changes.
- Confirm observability events are emitted with stable element IDs (not visual indices).
- Add unit/e2e tests for keyboard order and screenreader announcements in RTL and long-label locales.

Risk & mitigation:
- Risk: Changing DOM order may affect analytics selectors. Mitigation: provide stable data-element attributes and update analytics mapping in tandem.
- Risk: New color tokens may conflict with existing product color schemes. Mitigation: include token aliases and backward-compatibility variables in theme layer.

Testing notes (QA):
- Test keyboard-only navigation across dashboard routes in en-US, fr-FR, ar-SA using NVDA/VoiceOver and ChromeVox.
- Use Axe and pa11y runs on the dashboard with mocked locale strings of varying lengths.
- Color contrast tests using Design System contrast tool for each locale and token variant.

Notes on localization:
- Numeric formats (thousands separator, decimal) should use client-locale helpers so scrim labels do not wrap unpredictably.
- For Arabic (ar-SA), ensure numeric elements in timelines keep LTR numeric runs where needed and that RTL mirroring flips only layout flow, not numeric direction if requirement dictates.

Reference artifacts and links listed below.

title: Zero-downtime embedding dimension migration + client compatibility shim
preview:
Context:
We need to change the canonical dimensionality of text embeddings used by the reranking endpoint from 768 -> 1024 to support a new model family and improved quality. This ticket captures the migration plan, compatibility shims, cost/latency trade-offs, and rollout phases for zero-downtime reindexing and client impact mitigation.

Problem statement:
- Some customers embed and store vectors in customer indexes and call rerank endpoints that expect 768-dim vectors. Changing dims will break clients that send old vectors or clients that expect the API to return 768-dim vectors.
- Reindexing existing datasets is expensive and can cause prolonged degraded performance if done naively.

Goals:
1) Allow the backend to serve 1024-dim embeddings while remaining compatible with clients that still produce or expect 768-dim vectors.
2) Provide clear guidance and tooling for customers to reindex on their schedule with minimal service disruption.
3) Keep client-side changes minimal and provide an opt-in compatibility shim.
4) Monitor quality and latency regressions, with a rollback path.

Design summary (decisions made):
- Dual-index strategy: for each customer index we will create a parallel index that stores 1024-dim vectors while keeping the 768-dim index intact until reindex finishes. Reads route smartly based on client compatibility flags.
- Server-side projection adapter: implement a fast linear projection matrix on the server to map 768->1024 at query time for clients that still send 768-dim vectors. The projection is learned from a small calibration dataset and stored per-model-variant. This avoids immediate bulk reindexing.
- On-the-fly reindex worker: background job that converts stored 768 vectors to 1024 using the canonical model and writes to the new index; it uses incremental batching with throttles to avoid saturating I/O or GPUs.
- Client compatibility header: introduce X-Embedding-Dim or a versioned content-type (application/x-embedding;dim=768) so clients can explicitly declare vector dims. If not provided, server will infer and apply projection if configured.
- Migration flags: per-customer toggle to enable aggressive background reindexing or to remain in compatibility mode.

Trade-offs considered:
- Projection vs. reindex accuracy: projection is expedient but may not match full-model 1024 vectors in quality. We will surface quality delta metrics and recommend reindex for high-accuracy use cases.
- Cost: storing dual indexes temporarily increases storage and increases write amplification during migration. We accepted this for lower customer disruption.
- Latency: server-side projection adds a small CPU/GPU cost; we optimized to keep p50 impact minimal and p99 < 2ms added for typical requests. Benchmark details in artifacts below.

Migration plan (phases):
Phase 0 - Prep (complete): schema changes, new index templates, projection service skeleton, client header spec.
Phase 1 - Compatibility rollout (In Progress): enable projection adapter and compatibility header, open opt-in to customers for server-side projection.
Phase 2 - Controlled reindex (next 2-4 weeks): run on-the-fly background reindex for opted-in customers; monitor retrieval/rr precision and latencies; notify customers.
Phase 3 - Wide reindex + deprecation (after customer notice period): once most customers have reindexed or accepted projection, begin deprecation of 768-first-class support and keep a thin compatibility shim for legacy accounts during a defined sunset window.

Operational runbook highlights:
- Metrics to observe: reindex throughput (vectors/sec), background worker lag, index size growth, average and p99 rerank latency, rerank NDCG@10 vs baseline, projection error distribution (cosine similarity).
- Alerts: reindex worker failure rate > 1% / hour, projection CPU exceeds threshold on > 3 nodes, rerank p99 increased by > 30% vs baseline.
- Rollback: toggle customer to compatibility mode to switch reads back to 768 index and disable projection, pause background reindex.

Client guidance (what we will publish):
- Immediate: add X-Embedding-Dim header when sending vectors. If header is missing, server will infer but best practice is to declare dims.
- Short-term: upgrade client SDKs to call /embeddings with model parameter that returns 1024-dim vectors, follow reindex guide.
- Long-term: re-run embedding jobs with the new model and use the provided tooling to reindex into the canonical index.

Testing and quality gates:
- Unit/integration tests for projection adapter and per-model calibration loading.
- End-to-end QA harness that runs rerank on blue/green indexes with synthetic and customer-sampled queries to compute NDCG and MRR deltas.
- Canary rollout to 5 low-risk customers, then 20, then full fleet after meeting quality thresholds: NDCG drop < 0.5% and latency increase < 10% p99.

Links & artifacts:
- Design doc (internal): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/Embedding+Dim+Migration+Design
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/1425
- Background worker PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/embeddings/pull/621
- Benchmarks and dashboard: https://dashboards.redwood.ai/d/embeddings/migration-1024

Next steps (short):
- Finish projection calibration tuning (Mateo) by 2026-03-15.
- Add client SDK header support and test harness (DevX) by 2026-03-18.
- Begin canary reindex on two customers (opt-in) on 2026-03-20.

title: Safe automated batching/caching config rollouts with eval-driven golden-prompt acceptance
preview:
Background: We want to automatically recommend and apply batching/caching configuration changes to reduce per-token cost while avoiding quality regressions. Past attempts showed cost wins but introduced subtle output regressions for specific prompt patterns (notably multi-turn contexts and rare-entity prompts).

Goal: Implement an automated rollout pipeline that: (1) generates candidate batching/cache config changes, (2) validates them against a focused eval suite driven by golden prompts and task-specific metrics, (3) safely applies changes via staged rollouts (canary -> ramp) with automated rollback if acceptance thresholds fail, and (4) provides observability and post-mortem artifacts.

Scope: This ticket covers the eval gating system, golden prompt set curation, acceptance thresholds, canary spec, rollback runbook, and dashboarding hooks. Separate tickets will handle the UI in Console and the autoscaler integration.

Design summary:
- Candidate generation: cost-tuner produces candidate configs (batch size, KV cache TTL, prefix caching policies) and estimated cost delta.
- Eval gating: each candidate runs through the eval harness that executes a golden-prompt suite (seeded real prompts + synthetic edge cases) and computes a set of signal metrics.
- Metrics: primary metrics include response similarity (embedding cosine), task-specific accuracy where available, generation token delta, and hallucination regressions measured via an instruction-following checklist. Secondary metrics: end-to-end latency, P50/P95 token-time, and cost-per-request.
- Acceptance policy: a candidate passes iff all primary metrics are within configured absolute thresholds AND no more than X% of prompts show >Y% degradation. Thresholds are tunable per-model family.
- Rollout: successful candidate -> canary group (1% traffic) for 1 hour with continuous sampling and accumulated eval checks. If canary passes, ramp to 10% then 50% with checkpoints. Failing any checkpoint triggers automatic rollback and page to oncall.

Trade-offs considered:
- Strict thresholds reduce false positives but slow down cost savings. We balance by allowing model-family-specific thresholds and a separate permissive "exploratory" channel for teams to test aggressive optimizations.
- Running the full eval suite in production for every candidate is expensive; we split into a smaller real-time canary suite + offline full-suite batch run.

Notes on golden prompts: we will maintain two sets:
1) Core golden prompts (200): high-signal user-facing prompts pulled from historical production traces, deduplicated and labeled by intent.
2) Edge-case prompts (50): synthetic and adversarial prompts targeting hallucination, multi-turn state, long-context truncation, and uncommon entities.

Acceptance thresholds (initial defaults):
- Embedding cosine similarity >= 0.945 aggregated median across core set.
- Task accuracy drop <= 1.0 percentage point for labeled tasks.
- Hallucination rate delta <= +0.5 percentage points on edge set.
- Median response length delta within +/-10%.
- Cost reduction >= 6% (else don't promote unless explicit override).

Observability: integrate with Console to show per-candidate lens: cost delta, eval pass/fail broken down by prompt groups, top 10 failing prompts, and time-series of production-quality signals. Create a dedicated dashboard in Grafana referencing canary traces and eval harness outputs.

title: Soft-anchored capacity allocator for geo-failover routing
preview:
Purpose: implement a soft-anchored capacity allocator that combines latency-first routing with per-region capacity credits and probabilistic spillover to preserve SLOs during partial region degradation or failover events. The allocator should: (1) prefer geo-pinned endpoints when latency and credits permit; (2) gradually shift traffic away from an impacted region using a probabilistic hedging factor rather than an all-or-nothing cutover; (3) enforce circuit-breaker style backpressure and per-customer soft quotas to avoid overload on receiving regions; and (4) expose observability and controls in Console for operators to tune hedging fraction, credit refill rates, and emergency evacuation overrides.

Why: full hard failover leads to large tail spikes and cross-region overload (thundering herd) when an upstream region becomes degraded. A soft, capacity-aware approach reduces tail latency regression and provides smoother operator control during incidents.

Non-goals: this ticket does not change model variants or on-host batching logic; it focuses on request-level routing decisions and accompanying telemetry. On-prem private deployments will receive a compatible config surface but not the managed default rollout in this sprint.

title: Audit evidence packaging API and residency-change notification webhooks
preview:
Goal: Provide a single API and webhook surface that automates packaging and delivery of compliance evidence (audit logs, policy evaluations, retention actions) and notifies customers of data residency changes. The solution must: 1) produce tamper-evident evidence bundles (signed + SHA256) that can be retrieved or pushed to customer endpoints; 2) deliver residency-change events that include previous and target region metadata; 3) expose retention enforcement events with links to evidence artifacts; 4) support robust webhook delivery semantics (idempotency, retries, signature verification, batching).

API / Design summary:
- Evidence packaging API: POST /v1/compliance/evidence-generate
  - Request: {customer_id, timeframe_start, timeframe_end, include_logs: boolean, include_prompts: boolean, retention_policy_id}
  - Response: {evidence_id, status: queued|ready|failed, estimated_size_bytes}
  - When ready we emit evidence.ready webhook with evidence_url (presigned), evidence_sha256, bundle_size_bytes, kms_key_id, storage_region.

- Retention enforcement events: system evaluates retention policies and emits retention.enforced or retention.expired events. Each event includes: {policy_id, resource_ids, action: delete|anonymize|archive, evidence_id (if archive), ttl_days, effective_at}.

- Residency-change notifications: emitted when a customer's data residency setting changes or when an automatic reassignment happens for operational reasons. Event types: residency.change.requested, residency.change.completed, residency.change.failed. Payload includes: {customer_id, previous_region, requested_region, approved_by, reason, migration_plan_url, impact_summary}.

Webhook delivery guarantees & verification:
- Delivery: at-least-once. Consumers must use the Idempotency-Key header to dedupe; each event contains event_id and event_type.
- Signing: HMAC-SHA256 using X-Redwood-Signature header. Signature format: v1=<hex>. Service exposes public key endpoints for asymmetric verification for enterprise customers that require it.
- Retry/backoff: exponential backoff with jitter; max attempts configurable per subscription. Delivery status callbacks sent to console for SRE/ops monitoring.
- Batches: large evidence.ready events can be split into batch events with batch_index/batch_count. Bodies may be gzip-compressed; check Content-Encoding header.

Evidence bundle characteristics:
- Bundles are compressed tar.gz with JSON index (index.json) containing file list, SHA256 for each file, creation_ts, generator_version, KMS key_id used for envelope encryption.
- Artifact storage: evidence-bundles/<customer_id>/<evidence_id>/<bundle>.tar.gz in region matching data residency target. Presigned URLs valid for configurable TTL (default 3 days).
- Audit trail: each bundle also writes a small metadata record to the audit stream (audit.v1) with retention-policy metadata and checksum.

Operational behaviors & edge cases:
- If residency-change would move data across jurisdictions, we emit residency.change.requested and mark resources as migration_pending; evidence generation for affected resources is allowed but will be tagged previous_region until migration completes.
- For retention.enforced delete actions, we generate a retention.evidence bundle summarizing the deletions (what was deleted, hashes of previously stored objects, who requested the deletion) and attach it to the retention.enforced event.
- Idempotency: evidence.generate requests with the same client-supplied idempotency key will return the same evidence_id if within TTL.
- Failure modes: delivery failures create an internal incident and are surfaced to customers via console notifications and email if configured. For high-severity failures (evidence bundle corruption, signature mismatch in customer ingestion), escalate to on-call SRE.

Acceptance criteria:
- Customers can create webhook subscriptions scoped to event types and regions.
- Evidence bundles generated by API are retrievable via presigned URL and verify against provided SHA256.
- Residency change events include migration_plan_url and region metadata; console shows migration status.
- Webhooks include HMAC-SHA256 signature and support idempotency and retries.
- End-to-end tests for a sample enterprise account validating generation -> delivery -> retrieval -> verification.

Notes on privacy and encryption:
- Bundles encrypted with customer-specific envelope KMS keys when configured. Default method uses platform-managed KMS with region-specific keys.
- PII minimization: by default prompt/response content is redacted unless customer opts-in via policy flag.

title: Smart Batch Orchestrator: workload signatures, safety guards, and continuous window tuning
preview: Build a Smart Batch Orchestrator (SBO) that continuously recommends and safely applies batching and caching parameters per workload. SBO will profile live traffic to infer 'workload signatures' (inference footprint distribution, token length histograms, client concurrency patterns, and prompt prefix commonalities) and produce a ranked set of parameter changes (batch size, aggregation window, token-threshold, aggressive KV cache retention) together with an estimated cost delta and latency risk. The ticket covers design and a first-pass implementation for: (1) offline profiling + feature extraction, (2) a heuristic recommender that maps signatures to parameter candidates, (3) safe-apply primitives (canary split, dry-run simulation, rollback triggers), and (4) Console UI hooks for review and acceptance. The goal is automated, conservative recommendations that reduce per-token cost without violating latency SLOs or increasing tail errors.

title: Transient capacity cushion and billing smoothing policy for burst events
preview:
Problem: Customers on Dedicated frequently experience short-lived traffic spikes (minutes) that exceed reserved throughput. Current behaviors either (a) allow immediate overage invoicing with large cost spikes or (b) fail requests when autoscale is slow. Goal: introduce a transient capacity cushion (TCC) and a billing smoothing policy that provide predictable short-term burst handling without permanent changes to reserved capacity or surprising invoices.

Scope: design, server/runtime changes, billing pipeline adjustments, console UI, and operator controls. This ticket covers engineering design and initial implementation for the TCC core runtime and billing smoothing hook; downstream UI and docs tasks will be split as follow-ups.

Non-goals: permanent reservation changes, pricing redesign for long-term overages (>72 hours), or self-serve purchase flows for burst bundles (separate PM work).

title: Recency-aware length-bucketed sampler for per-tenant calibration packs
preview: Goal: Define and implement a reproducible sampling strategy to generate per-tenant (per-domain) calibration packs for FP8/INT8 quantization validation. The sampler must preserve production distribution properties relevant to inference quality while keeping pack sizes bounded for repeatable calibration runs. Key constraints: (1) production prompt traffic is heavy-tailed across intents and tenants, (2) KV-cache and sequence length distributions affect quantization error, (3) datasets must be de-duped and privacy-safe, and (4) we need deterministic seeds and manifest metadata for later auditing.

title: Spiral rollout orchestration with probe-matrix checks and fallthrough routing lanes
preview:
Goal: implement a 'spiral' rollout primitive that layers A/B slices, shadow probes, and progressive traffic promotion with deterministic fallthrough lanes by region and capacity tier. The spiral flow exercises a probe-matrix for compatibility checks (input shapes, output schema, kv-cache parity, streaming semantics) before live traffic promotion. Fallthrough lanes define ordered routing fallback: local warm-variant -> regional-compatible-variant -> low-cost-quantized-variant -> hosted-fallback. We want deterministic, auditable routing decisions and clear automatic rollback triggers based on end-to-end quality signals and capacity SLO breaches.

Scope: routing policy composer, probe-matrix evaluation engine, fallthrough lane config schema, rollout controller changes, metric hooks for quality SLOs, canary traffic UI in Console, and docs for on-call. Excludes long-term rollout analytics pipeline and cross-customer experiment UI (separate follow-up).

title: Invocation shims for cross-SDK function/tool calls with retry idempotency and streaming ergonomics
preview: Implement language idiomatic invocation shims across Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs that unify function/tool schema validation, partial/streaming function responses, and retry-safe invocation semantics. Goal is to provide: (1) a lightweight FunctionInvoker helper in each SDK that coerces and validates user-supplied arguments against model-declared schemas, (2) idempotency and dedup tokens to make retries safe across network failures and streaming interruptions, and (3) ergonomic streaming APIs that surface partial results and function invocation progress with minimal boilerplate. This ticket covers design choices, cross-language examples, and initial implementation for the Python and TypeScript SDKs with an engineering spike for Go ergonomics.

title: Tenant slot autoscaler + soft-QoS shaper for embeddings ingress
preview:
Goal: Reduce tail latency and increase predictable throughput for the embeddings ingestion pipeline by introducing a tenant-slot based autoscaler and a soft-QoS shaper that gracefully degrades non-critical requests during congestion. Scope includes controller design, ingress admission changes, metrics, and rollout plan.

Background: Current embedding endpoints suffer unpredictable p99 increases under multi-tenant bursts and have limited tenant-level controls. Existing work focuses on queue smoothing and provisioner strategies; this ticket targets an ingress-focused solution that prevents single-tenant bursts from destabilizing global pools while preserving throughput and quality for high-priority traffic.

Proposed solution:
- Implement a tenant-slot abstraction: each tenant is allocated a number of concurrency slots that map to admission tokens at the ingress router. Slots are elastic: the autoscaler increases slots for tenants with sustained healthy success/latency signals and decreases when idle.
- Soft-QoS shaper: when capacity contention is detected, non-critical embedding requests are shifted to a best-effort lane with longer timeouts and higher batching thresholds, instead of hard rejecting. Critical lanes (paid tiers, SLAs, internal control traffic) keep strict latency SLOs.
- Admission and backpressure: admission control will use a token-bucket per-tenant derived from slots plus a global reservoir. Excess requests are queued in a short-lived in-memory window (max 150ms) before entering best-effort lane.
- Autoscaler heuristics: use EWMA of observed p50/p95, success rates, and queue occupancy to decide slot changes. Include safety caps (max growth rate, cooldown).
- Observability: new metrics for slots_allocated_by_tenant, slot_utilization, best_effort_fallback_rate, per-tenant p50/p95/p99, queue_drain_time. Dashboards and alerts for unexpected fallback spikes.
- Control plane: expose per-tenant overrides in Console (initially feature-flagged) to pin slots or set QoS tier.

Acceptance criteria: (see acceptance_criteria field)

Rollout plan: feature-flag behind rollout config. Start with internal tenants + low-traffic set in staging. Canary to 5% production traffic (non-critical tenants). Monitor p99 and best_effort_fallback_rate for 72h. Gradual increase with rollback on >10% p99 regression or >2x fallback for critical tenants.

Risks and mitigations:
- Over-aggressive scale-down could starve jobs: mitigation: conservative downscale rate and grace period.
- Complexity + bugs in admission logic could cause inequitable scheduling: mitigation: enable simulation mode and shadow decisions for one week, run tenant-level fairness tests.
- Measurement noise causing oscillation: mitigation: smoothing windows and minimum slot size.

Success metrics:
- Reduce embedding endpoint p99 for at-risk tenants by >=20% during bursts.
- Keep overall error rate for critical lanes <= baseline + 0.5%.
- Limit best-effort fallback rate to <5% of total requests after warm-up.

title: cold-kv-sojourn-eviction-watermark-probe-and-graceful-preempt-framework
preview: Introduce a conservative, low-latency preemption and fairness framework for continuous batching that explicitly accounts for KV cache residency (hot vs cold sojourns) and GPU memory fragmentation. Goals: reduce tail latency regressions for high-priority tenants, bound customer impact when eviction is necessary, and avoid repeated cold-start churn for recently accessed KV entries.

title: Sequence Slab Prioritizer & Padding Trimmer for Prefill/Decode
preview:
Goal: reduce padded tokens across prefill+decode micro-batches while preserving per-request P50 latency SLO and avoiding kernel thrashing. We propose a Sequence Slab Prioritizer that groups requests into compact 'slabs' by seq-len quantiles and kernel-affinity hints, combines a lightweight prefix coalescer for short-prefill-heavy traffic, and a pad-trimmer pass that opportunistically strips tail padding during GPU kernel invocation. Success metrics: 20-35% padded-token reduction in mixed production traffic, <=5% regression on P50 latency for smallest tier, and 0 net increase in kernel switch-count per second.

Design highlights:
- Quantile slab construction: calculate online seqlen quantiles (16/32/64/128 buckets) per 1s window and form slabs that balance token volume vs slab-count.
- Kernel affinity hints: annotate slabs with preferred kernel families (FP16/INT8/FlashAttention variant) using a compact model-profile table to avoid suboptimal kernel selection at launch.
- Prefill-aware coalescer: for requests where prefill dominates, coalesce prefixes into a shared KV cache warm-up path to amortize compute and reduce padding for subsequent decodes.
- Pad-trimmer kernel wrapper: a thin runtime wrapper that applies run-length compression of trailing padding and issues trimmed kernel calls; for kernels that support variable-length execution, avoid allocation penalties by reusing slab buffers.

Trade-offs and risks:
- More aggressive slab splitting reduces padding but increases count of kernel launches. We mitigate with kernel affinity aggregation and a launch budget heuristic.
- Prefill coalescing adds jitter for single-request tails; will gate by request latency delta threshold and customer tier.
- Kernel wrapper complexity may expose edge cases with custom kernels; plan a compatibility matrix and fail-open behavior.

Rollout plan:
1) Smoke experiments in internal perf-cluster with synthetic seqlen distributions (short-heavy, bimodal, long-tail) and kernel profiling.
2) Canary in hosted API: 1% traffic for non-enterprise orgs with metrics dashboard and automatic rollback on SLO breach.
3) Gradual ramp with step-down thresholds and opt-out for Dedicated customers.

Observability and gating:
- New dashboard: slab_count, padded_token_ratio, kernel_switches_per_s, per-slab P50/P95 latency, slab_affinity_mismatch_rate.
- Alerting rules: padded_token_ratio > +15% regression (roll back), kernel_switches_per_s > +25% baseline.

Artifacts and links:
- Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/RUNTIME/Sequence+Slab+Prioritizer
- Perf experiment notebook: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1X-sample-notebook
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/4321
- Dashboard (Grafana): https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/seq-slab/seq-slab-overview

Next steps:
- Implement slab builder and kernel-affinity table (week 1)
- Integrate pad-trimmer wrapper and bench kernels (week 2)
- Run end-to-end perf experiments and create canary rollout plan (week 3)

title: Dynamic memory cadence for shared GPU safety
preview:
Context: Serving runtime must allocate GPU memory across model weights, KV cache, and activations while supporting continuous batching. Current static headroom allocations cause either wasted reserved memory or frequent OOMs during bursty traffic. Goal: implement a time-cadenced headroom recalculation and transactional per-tenant soft budgets that reconcile with the KV eviction/aging policy to improve utilization and reduce OOM rate.

Scope: algorithm and runtime changes in the scheduler and memory allocator; telemetry and SLOs; migration plan for rolling changes across running hosts. Not in scope: model weight compression (separate project).

title: Admission Moderator: resilience for quota controller outages, burst pacing and multi-region failover
preview: Problem statement: Recent incidents show our central quota controller can become a single point of pain during regional network partitions and flash bursts. When the limiter fabric loses the lease or RPCs stall, customers observe long tail latencies, opaque 429 spikes, and cascading retries that violate token-level SLOs. This ticket designs and implements an Admission Moderator layer that: 1) provides degraded but fair admission when quota store is partially unavailable, 2) enforces burst pacing to avoid downstream stalls, 3) integrates with multi-region circuit-breakers and prioritized shedding policies, and 4) emits telemetry needed for SLO guardrails and automated rollback triggers.

title: Concilium: token-coalescer for quantized kernel alignment
preview: Implement a token-coalescing runtime layer (Concilium) that reorganizes prefills and microbatches to maximize reuse for INT8/FP8-friendly blocked kernels. The goal is to reduce dequantization overhead and improve kernel alignment for attention and KV prefill paths without changing model semantics. Concilium will: 1) coalesce adjacent token slices across streams where safe, 2) introduce a headwise remap metadata layer so blocked kernels can operate on a coalesced memory layout, 3) provide lazy dequantization views to avoid materializing full FP tensors when not needed, and 4) coordinate prefill scheduling so that coalesced blocks are filled and decoded with minimal synchronization overhead.

title: Service degradation catalog and incident autopsy canvas
preview:
Goal: Create a compact, actionable catalog of service degradation signatures and a linked visual "autopsy canvas" that lets on-call and SRE quickly map from symptom -> telemetry -> probable root cause. This ticket covers taxonomy definition, schema for trace/log enrichment, required metric counters, and three Grafana dashboards (Degradation Map, Error Slice Explorer, Incident Autopsy Canvas).

Scope:
- Define failure signatures for timeouts, overloaded workers, model-level failures (OOM, decode errors, generation truncation), HTTP/gRPC codes and proxies, client-side aborts, and retry-induced amplification.
- Propose standardized trace/log fields and enum values (e.g. redwood.error_class, redwood.model_status, redwood.slo_bucket) and a migration plan for instrumented services.
- Implement tracing hooks in runtime that add fields when failures are observed and propagate trace_id through gateways.
- Wire enriched spans to metrics/log pipelines (Prometheus histograms, counter labels; logs to Loki/Elastic with structured fields).
- Build dashboards and alert rules; verify with injection test harness.

Constraints & trade-offs:
- We avoid full-fidelity distributed sampling for initial rollout due to storage costs; instead we implement adaptive sampling (retain 100% for high-severity signatures, 1% baseline otherwise).
- Schema must be compact for label cardinality limits: prefer coarse enums with optional freeform tags only attached to sampled traces.

Success metrics: Reduced time-to-root-cause for P1 inference degradations from current median of 28m to <10m; reduction in duplicate incident pages by 30% within first quarter after rollout.

title: Golden-output test harness for typed SDK responses and streaming retry semantics
preview: Create a reproducible 'golden output' test harness for the Python SDK that exercises typed response models (pydantic + dataclasses), streaming chunk reassembly, and retry/idempotency behaviors. The harness should produce deterministic fixtures that CI can validate across platforms and Python versions, provide a pytest plugin for test ergonomics, and include example end-to-end tests that simulate transient network errors, backpressure, and resumed streams with checkpoints. This will help prevent regressions in structured output parsing, streaming hydration, and retry policies when changing SDK parsing logic or runtime semantics.

title: Graceful rate-limit throttling and credit-repair for active streaming sessions
preview:
Summary:

Active streaming sessions that encounter server-side rate-limiting (429s) or abrupt in-flight throttles can produce inconsistent billing outcomes: clients may be recharged for provisional token credits, or conversely, fail to receive credit for fragments that were generated but never finalized. This ticket implements a conservative, auditable mechanism to (1) throttle streams gracefully to avoid orphan charges, (2) track provisional credits during throttles, and (3) perform automated credit-repair/reconciliation when sessions either resume or are abandoned.

Problem details and motivators:
- Observed during high-concurrency incident on 2026-02-28: when the regional rate limiter injected backpressure, ~0.7% of active streaming sessions ended in a midstream 429 followed by client retry. Billing ledger showed either double-charges (provisional + final) or lost credits depending on resume timing.
- Complexities: streaming responses are incremental (partial fragments). Clients may perform resume semantics (idempotent resume) or reconnect with new session IDs. Rate-limit induced pauses create windows where we cannot safely finalize token counts.

Goals:
- Avoid double-billing or lost charges due to midstream throttles while preserving low-latency failover for clients.
- Provide clear audit trail linking provisional credits to final reconciliation outcomes.
- Keep implementation incremental and reversible behind feature flag.

Design proposal (high level):
1) Provisional credit window: on first throttle event for a session, mark outstanding fragments as PROVISIONAL instead of FINAL on the in-memory ledger. Emit metric: meter.provisional_count.
2) Throttle-holder lease: create short-lived lease object on the session that records throttled_offset, provisional_tokens, lease_expiry. This prevents immediate finalization and duplicates when client reconnects.
3) Resume semantics: when a client resumes, server verifies resume id + lease. If resume matches, convert provisional -> final up to confirmed offset. If resume is not recognized (new session), enqueue reconciliation job to determine which provisional credits should be kept, refunded, or merged.
4) Reconciliation pass: background worker scans leases that expired or sessions marked ABANDONED and performs heuristics: compare persisted transcripts + KV cache to claim final tokens where possible; otherwise roll back provisional credits and issue negative adjustments. All adjustments are written to ledger with provenance tags.
5) Safeguards: pessimistic limits for provisional exposure per account (max_provisional_tokens) and a global circuit-breaker to avoid large provisional debt during outages.

Trade-offs considered:
- Accuracy vs Complexity: aggressive provisional logic increases correctness but adds background reconciliation complexity and more long-tail edge cases. We prefer correctness for billing (customer trust) while limiting provisional exposure by default.
- Performance: adding leases and extra reconciliation tasks increases DB IO. We'll implement leases in a fast KV table (Redis) and only persist reconciliation results to the ledger service.

Telemetry and observability:
- New dashboard: Billing > Streaming > Throttled Sessions (grafana link). Key metrics: provisional_tokens, provisional_sessions, reconciliations_per_min, reconciliation_latency, reclaimed_tokens, refunded_tokens.
- Alerts: provisional_tokens > 5% of active tokens for >5m triggers PagerDuty to oncall-metrics.

Testing plan:
- Unit tests for lease lifecycle, provisional->final path, and rollback logic.
- Integration tests: simulate induced 429s with varied resume behaviors (idempotent resume, reconnect new id, abandoned).
- Load test: chaos-run with 25k concurrent streams for 30m with intermittent throttles and confirm reconciliation throughput and ledger correctness.

Rollout plan:
1) Feature-flag the provisional flow for 10% of traffic in a single region.
2) Monitor metrics and run reconciliation dry-run (compute adjustments without writing ledger) for 48h.
3) If metrics stable, enable full reconciliation writes and expand to 50% then global.
4) Final: turn on default safeguards (max_provisional_tokens per account).

Open design decisions:
- Heuristic precedence: prefer finalizing tokens when transcript matches a persisted final fragment vs refunding. Need PM/legal guidance for disputed charges.
- Reconciliation TTL: currently 24h lease expiry; could be shortened or extended based on ops load.

Artifacts and links:
- Design doc (Confluence): https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/METERING/pages/GRACEFUL+THROTTLE+DESIGN
- Dashboard (Grafana): https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/streaming-throttle
- Incident notes: https://redwood.incidents/2026-02-28-throttle-incident

title: Operational Templates: parameterized admin workflows + automation harness
preview:
Problem: Our current admin utilities are a mixture of ad-hoc scripts, shell commands in runbooks, and multiple handcrafted Slack shortcuts. This causes knowledge silos, inconsistent execution, and poor auditability when support engineers perform common maintenance tasks. We frequently see follow-up bug churn when manual steps are missed or parameters are wrong.

Goal: Deliver a lightweight operational template system and an automation harness that lets support and SRE staff run parameterized admin workflows as first-class tasks. Templates should: (1) be source-controlled (Git + templates directory), (2) expose safe default parameters with explicit confirmations, (3) create a discrete task instance that is auditable and trackable in the Ops dashboard, and (4) optionally wire into automated steps (scripts) or human-driven checkpoints.

Proposed solution: 3 components: 
1) Template schema and validator - a small declarative format (YAML/JSON) that defines inputs, validation rules, pre-checks, estimated impact, and an ordered list of steps. Each step can be one of: shell action, HTTP API call (internal Admin API), or manual approval. Templates live in a git repo under infra/ops-templates and are validated by CI.
2) Orchestration harness (ops-harness) - a lightweight service that instantiates templates as Task records, runs programmatic steps in a sandboxed execution environment, posts structured progress to Slack/console, and records results in the audit log. The harness will enforce RBAC and environment safeguards (dry-run by default for high-impact templates).
3) Console + Slack integration - a minimal UI in Console for selecting templates, supplying inputs, and watching progress. Slack commands should be available for starting low-risk templates and for fetching task status.

Success metrics: 
- 75% of commonly executed admin actions migrated to templates within 8 weeks.
- Reduction of human-error incidents from admin tasks by 60% measured over next quarter.
- All template runs have structured audit logs and links to task instances in Console.

Trade-offs and constraints: 
- We avoid building a full workflow engine (e.g., temporal) for this MVP — target is a focused, ops-oriented runner with explicit steps and limited persistence. This reduces complexity but limits long-running complex orchestrations.
- Security posture: high-impact templates require a secondary approval and must be audited; this adds friction to urgent fixes. We'll provide an emergency bypass process tied to SRE rota with extra logging.
- Execution environment: initial runner will use a dedicated short-lived ephemeral container on our internal infra rather than launching on customer-facing hosts. This avoids accidentally touching customer data.

Implementation plan (milestones): 
- Week 1: Template schema definition, validator, and example templates for cold reboot, model-cache flush, Redis rolling restart. (owner: Mateo)
- Week 2: Ops-harness prototype to instantiate a task, execute HTTP steps, and post progress to Slack. (owner: Srivatsan)
- Week 3: RBAC enforcement, auditing hooks (CloudWatch/Datadog event), and dry-run mode. (owner: Aisha)
- Week 4: Console UI wire and usability session with Support; 3 templates migrated and internal beta. (owner: Design + SE)

title: Adaptive slot governor: simulating microbatch headroom and backpressure under sustained high QPS
preview:
Problem: At sustained high QPS spikes we observe repeated short-lived GPU memory pressure events that cause aggressive KV cache evictions and cascading microbatch thrashing. Existing admission heuristics use fixed thresholds tuned to average load, which either under-utilize GPUs or cause elevated p99 latency and eviction storms.

Goal: Implement an "adaptive slot governor" that continuously tunes microbatch admission thresholds and soft backpressure signals using a combination of short-window EWMA of queue depth, KV hit/evict rates, and a lightweight GPU headroom estimator. We will validate the governor in an offline simulator and a staged canary, instrumenting new telemetry to measure stability and cost impact.

Scope: This ticket covers design, simulator experiments, telemetry/alerting additions, and a staged runtime rollout strategy to test the governor's behavior under synthetic and production-like high-QPS traces. Implementation in runtime will be limited to a feature-flagged path and a default conservative policy for rollout.

Why different from prior tickets: Prior work focused on fixed hysteresis, precomputed thresholds, or token-accounting governors. This approach treats slot allocation as a continuous control problem with smoothing and lookback windows, explicitly modeling KV eviction dynamics and GPU memory reclamation latency to avoid oscillation.
