title: Predictive turn-bundling coordinator with horizon-based KV prefetch for streaming chat
preview: Implement a coordinator that forms ephemeral bundles of streaming chat turns using a predictive horizon window. The coordinator will group turns from in-flight sessions into short-lived "bundles" when the model and KV cache predictions indicate overlapping KV slot access or contiguous token generation, enabling larger continuous batches without adding tail latency for actively typing users. The approach combines a short predictive horizon (50-300ms), per-session priority scores, and a progressive KV prefetch strategy that warms only the most-likely KV slots to stay within per-GPU slab budgets.

title: Slate tile packer: adaptive tile-based batch formation with KV-tiling and GPU budget enforcement for streaming chat
preview: Problem: For streaming chat completions we need a batch formation strategy that minimizes tail latency while maximizing GPU utilization and respecting a strict per-GPU memory budget. Existing continuous-batching approaches either over-consolidate multi-turn sessions (increasing KV cache pressure) or under-bundle short-turn traffic (wasting kernel throughput). This ticket proposes a new 'slate tile packer' heuristic that treats incoming streaming requests as tiles placed into a bounded slate per worker, with explicit KV-tiling and GPU memory budgeting to avoid OOMs and excessive KV thrashing.

title: Python SDK: Structured model helpers and streaming ergonomics quickstart
preview:
Goal: ship a small, opinionated set of helpers in the Python SDK that make it trivial to 1) declare typed response models (dataclass + optional Pydantic validation), 2) hydrate partial/streamed responses into typed objects as chunks arrive, and 3) provide ergonomic retry and backpressure hooks for streaming consumers. This ticket is the engineering carrier for an end-to-end quickstart that will be used in docs and the examples repo.

Background: we have many internal patterns across runtime and examples that parse streaming JSON or chunked 'delta' events into complete objects. Teams want a supported SDK surface that reduces boilerplate and avoids reimplementing fragile merge/hydration logic. This should integrate with existing Python SDK streaming iterators and respect retry semantics (idempotency, resumption), plus make it easy to opt into Pydantic for stricter validation when users want it.

Non-goals: providing exhaustive schema generation from model weights, building a runtime-level retry/resume protocol, or supporting every 3rd-party validation lib. We provide a small surface and guidance for extensibility.

title: KV cache eviction regression & poison-susceptibility follow-up
preview:
Summary: During a production capacity run on 2026-02-28 we observed repeated latency spikes and degraded throughput across several hosted clusters. Root cause analysis identified a compound failure mode in the KV/prefix cache: an eviction heuristic regression combined with an edge-case in cache key normalization allowed stale/duplicated entries to remain referenced, causing memory growth and periodic cache poisoning (incorrect KV lookup results for hot prefixes). This ticket tracks follow-ups and action items from the postmortem aiming to fully remediate the regression, add guardrails, and close monitoring gaps.

What happened: A change in the eviction score calculation (PR #1984) altered LRU aging for small shard segments. Under typical workloads this increased cache churn, but under a specific prefix distribution we saw double-insert races where identical logical keys were stored with different internal representations (normalization mismatch). Eviction then removed the newer entry while the older, semantically incorrect entry remained reachable through a stale pointer in the prefix index. Over hours this caused memory to grow and some requests to return mismatched KV payloads (cache poisoning).

Impact: Intermittent elevated tail latency, errors in a small subset of requests returning outdated embeddings for cached prompts, and elevated memory usage leading to several autoscaler-triggered node restarts in affected Dedicated clusters. Customer-facing impact was limited but reproducible in internal load tests; severity classified as P0 for platform stability.

Mitigations already applied: rolled back PR #1984, disabled the new normalization path behind a feature flag, and deployed a short-term GC sweep job to reclaim orphaned entries (runbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/runbooks/kv-cache-orphan-sweep). Immediate pressure subsided after rollback but the underlying correctness and observability gaps remain.

title: Compact template-app onboarding: reference screenshots & README flow diagrams
preview:
Goal: Produce a compact set of reference screenshots and a minimal README flow diagram for the Template Apps quickstart that fit within the new docs compact layout.

Context:
- The docs team is introducing a condensed quickstart card for template apps used on the /quickstarts page. We need 6–8 annotated screenshots (desktop + one mobile highlight) and a single-page README flow diagram that explains first-run behavior and environment variables.
- Target audience: developers trying Redwood Inference with a template app (Node + Next.js starter). Focus on clarity, minimal copy, and visual parity with existing console UI (rollouts, model selector, logs).

Deliverables (acceptance criteria):
1) 6 annotated reference screenshots exported at 1440px wide (PNG) and 640px wide (mobile crop) that include: 1) repo clone + .env checklist, 2) local dev server running with Redwood token input modal, 3) console: model selector and latency-cost badge, 4) first call response showing streaming output, 5) rollout toggle + canary sample, 6) telemetry panel with token cost breakdown. Screens should be consistent with Figma component library and use Redwood colors/typography.
2) Single-page README flow diagram (SVG + PNG) that maps: Clone -> Install -> Set ENV -> Start -> Console Connect -> Make First Call -> View Telemetry. Diagram must be export-friendly for docs (800px height).
3) A README snippet (md) with inline images and alt text, copy limited to 120 characters per step.
4) A small captioned gallery for the quickstart card (3 images: hero, streaming, telemetry) sized for fast CDN delivery.

Notes on approach/constraints:
- Keep overlays minimal: use semi-transparent labels for key UI elements rather than full UI mockups. Avoid exposing any real API keys in images.
- Use generic sample content in responses (no PHI).
- Mobile crops should center on the chat stream and model selector for legibility.
- Follow accessibility contrast guidelines for annotation text (AA).

Progress updates / decisions / feedback log:
- 2026-02-12 (Aisha): Draft Figma file created: https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD-TEMPLATE-ONBOARDING (internal). Started with 8 screenshot frames.
- 2026-02-18 (Mateo): Reduced scope to 6 reference screens + 3-card gallery to speed up handoff for docs. Added acceptance criteria for image sizes.
- 2026-02-22 (PM - Lina Chen): Confirmed content scope with PM. Added explicit step for telemetry panel. PM requested copy that emphasizes cost transparency.
- 2026-02-26 (Engineering - Javier Ortiz): Flagged that streaming response state may differ locally; recommended using recorded sample JSON and static snapshot to avoid flakiness. Added link to sample response: https://github.com/redwood-inference/examples/blob/main/template-apps/sample_responses/streaming.json
- 2026-03-03 (Design review): Design review notes from Olivia Park: reduce annotation density, increase label font to 12px, and ensure rollouts toggle state is visible at 2x. Action: implement updates and export new assets.

text:
Goal: Produce a compact set of reference screenshots and a minimal README flow diagram for the Template Apps quickstart that fit within the new docs compact layout.

Context:
- The docs team is introducing a condensed quickstart card for template apps used on the /quickstarts page. We need 6–8 annotated screenshots (desktop + one mobile highlight) and a single-page README flow diagram that explains first-run behavior and environment variables.
- Target audience: developers trying Redwood Inference with a template app (Node + Next.js starter). Focus on clarity, minimal copy, and visual parity with existing console UI (rollouts, model selector, logs).

Deliverables (acceptance criteria):
1) 6 annotated reference screenshots exported at 1440px wide (PNG) and 640px wide (mobile crop) that include: 1) repo clone + .env checklist, 2) local dev server running with Redwood token input modal, 3) console: model selector and latency-cost badge, 4) first call response showing streaming output, 5) rollout toggle + canary sample, 6) telemetry panel with token cost breakdown. Screens should be consistent with Figma component library and use Redwood colors/typography.
2) Single-page README flow diagram (SVG + PNG) that maps: Clone -> Install -> Set ENV -> Start -> Console Connect -> Make First Call -> View Telemetry. Diagram must be export-friendly for docs (800px height).
3) A README snippet (md) with inline images and alt text, copy limited to 120 characters per step.
4) A small captioned gallery for the quickstart card (3 images: hero, streaming, telemetry) sized for fast CDN delivery.

Notes on approach/constraints:
- Keep overlays minimal: use semi-transparent labels for key UI elements rather than full UI mockups. Avoid exposing any real API keys in images.
- Use generic sample content in responses (no PHI).
- Mobile crops should center on the chat stream and model selector for legibility.
- Follow accessibility contrast guidelines for annotation text (AA).

title: Improve table interaction parity and runtime performance
preview:
Problem: Our table component has diverging behavior across density modes and struggles with large datasets. Users reported janky column-resize interactions, inconsistent keyboard focus when using virtualized rows, and confusing filter/sort affordances when density is compact. This ticket scopes a design and token update to deliver interaction parity across density modes, cleaner resize affordances, and layouts that enable engineering to implement efficient virtualization (variable row heights + sticky headers).

Goals:
- Establish consistent resize handles & hit targets across compact/comfortable/expanded density tokens.
- Define keyboard and screen-reader flow for column resize, reordering, and sorting that works with virtualization.
- Create visual and microinteraction specs for sorting and multi-column filters that scale to narrow column widths.
- Provide updated spacing/color/icon tokens to reduce layout thrashing and enable engineering batching/caching optimizations.

Scope excludes: server-side filtering UX, data modeling, and backend pagination behavior (handled by ENG-2101).

title: Welcome carousel + progressive disclosure for Dedicated capacity setup
preview: Design a compact entry experience for teams starting a Dedicated capacity trial that introduces key decisions (capacity sizing, autoscale policy, SLA tier) using a short welcome carousel and progressive disclosure panels. The flow should reduce cognitive load for first-time users by surfacing: a 3-card carousel that explains what Dedicated is, a capacity peek UI that visualizes throughput vs cost, an autoscale policy composer with example presets, and an SLA ribbon showing latency/uptime tradeoffs. Deliverables: high-fidelity screens for the console onboarding modal, a microcopy library for each panel, interactive prototype for user testing, and a small screenshot pack for release notes and docs.
